to discuss the kind of food that suits the slaves, and as for
Seneca, it was on a golden desk that he wrote his pages on
the beauty of poverty If mankind had followed their teach
ing, you would be to-day a black mule-driver in Central A
merica or a yellow-skinned humpback Chinese coolie inAtead
of a hopeless garbler of cheap literary junk."
Roar of laughter. One of the drummers turning towards
another; "Did'nt I tell you that you can't beat these priests?
They are the limit." "you bet your suspenders they are,"
rejoins the other.
"Well.. ..er.. ..that may be," retorted Ingersoli, "but we
agnpstics believe in virtue for its own sake, you Christians
to avoid hell and go to heaven."
"Then why don't you work free of charge for your fellow
men and stay, at home, instead of making money to enjoy ho
lidays? By the way,I see you are smoking a citar. You know
that Uncle Sam has collected revenue on that. Suppose he
made. taxes on tobacco optional, relying on the enlightened
good-will of the people for their payment. Suppose oun
laws were not enforced in the shape of -solid prison-bars and
the gallows, what would happen?
"Why, that would be anarchy. You cannot rely on the
people If he-did not see to it that the law is observed,.
Uncle Sam would be a consummate fool."
"And you are making one out of your own self just now, my.
pcor friend. If the wise law-maker has to enforce the law
and you cannot rely on the people, then, out of your o.wn
mouth, there must be a heaven and a hell."
"Hi, hi, hi ha, ha, hal you are caught, old man1" ex
claim the drummers, "you are caught in your own trap. You hadi
better tackle another job. Here, have a, drink and cheer up
for the next round."
Ingersoli declined the Kentucky and scratched his head
instead. Then he began to mumble the following:
"You Catholics are hopeless visionaries, any way. You, spend1.

title: Evening star. [volume]

text:
They gave us plenty.
Pearl Harbor... Wake... Bataan .. .Corregidor
... Java Sea ... Savo Island—the worst string of
defeats in America’s military history.
The Stars and Stripes hauled down on American
soil and the Rising Sun run up ... captured Ameri
can marines made to play coolie and haul grinning
Jap officers in rickshas—the Sons of Heaven have
handed us the bitterest medicine this nation ever
had to swallow.
NOW—LET’S GET ’EM!
Let’s pour it on them! Let’s shove the men, the
ships, the guns, and the planes over there in an
overwhelming, irresistible, avenging flood that will
wind up this Jap business fast, once and for all!
And let’s pay for that flood with the same grim
eagerness, the same deadly purpose that bur fight
ing men show in battle!
The 6th War Loan Drive is on. Let’s open our
purses—turn out our pockets—empty the sock.
Now, if never before, let’s make sure that we
buy at least one extra $100 War Bond.
From the pilot over Tokyo to the last housewife
on the Eastern Seaboard, let’s all of us, one hun
dred thirty million strong, rise up and give the
Sons of Heaven . . . hell!
And here are 6 more reasons for buying at least
an extra $100 War Bond in the 6th!
1. War Bonds are the best, the safest investment in the
world!
2. War Bonds return you $4 for every $3 at maturity.
\ 3. War Bonds are as convenient as cash—and increase in
value to boot!
4. War Bonds mean education for your children, security
for you, a nest egg to fall back on, come what may.
5. War Bonds help keep down the cost of living.
6. War Bonds will help win the Peace by increasing pur
chasing power after the war.

The Sheer Coats
For Summer
By Eleni Sakes
Fashion Editor
THE FASHION for sheer coats is making head
way. When worn with the new "vertical
line” sheath dresses, they make for striking en
sembles.
Coats of such delicate fabrics as chiffon, or
ganza and organdy help focus attention not only
on the garments fashioned of them, but also on the
prints and chiffon dresses worn underneath.
But news in the coat realm is not restricted
to sheer coat toppings alone. Cotton and linen
coats, crisp silk coats and coats of vividly colored
corduroy are gaining popularity where summery
chic is involved.
You’ll find each designer will have his own
original approach to the "warmer weather” coat.
Whether difference is achieved via fabric or design
ing technique, the fact is that the fabric coat
fashion launched well over two years ago is hitting
its stride this season.

text:
Thursday, March 11, 1943
Hawaii Not Safe
From Japs Yet,
Nimitz Warns
} HONOLULU, March 11 (INS)—
Japan still has the power to at
tempt a destructive raid on Hawaii
if she is wilting to take the risk.
Admiral Nimitz, Pacific fleet com-
mander, warned at ceremonies
marking the partial replacement
of the territory’s military gover
norship with a civil government.
“During the year,” said Ni
mitz, “thlo citadel of the Pacific
has been greatly strengthened—
which la In keeping with Its Im
portance.
“Ho waver, despite thla
strengthening and deaplte our
successes against the enemy, he
•till has the capacity to attempt
a destructive raid against our In
stallations if he Is willing to
accent the risk. Therefore, It Is

Japs Get Help of Conquered Peoples
(Continued from Page One)
thousands upon thousands of the natives of the
Japanese occupied countries.
One case cited was where, under expert Japanese direc
tion, natives quickly restored the production of oil wells
which, it had been believed, were destroyed before the
Japanese took over. These oil wells now are producing the
oil which Japan badly needed.
Another case was cited of where Japanese are assem
bling and even building airplanes in a territory dangerously
close to Australia. Japan does not have to send reinforce
ments to its armies in the South Pacific all the way from
Japan. She is getting these reinforcements from a place
much closer to the scenes of battle.
Japan has virtually cornered the world’s supply of
quinine, which is needed in the islands in the South Pacific
where our marines, soldiers and airmen are writing a glori
ous chapter in American history.
Attribute Success to Slogan
This co-operation the Japanese are getting from the
natives of the conquered countries is ascribed by American
military leaders to the long campaign the Japanese waged
for years before the world was shocked 6n Dec. 7, 1941, by
the Pearl Harbor attack. The Japs, these leaders tell you,
built the idea that these natives would be much better off
under Japanese control. “Asia for the Asiatics” was much
more than a slogan. It was a rallying point that enabled
Japan quickly to organize the occupied countries and bring
about production on a big scale of tin in Malaya, rubber
and oil in the Dutch East Indies now are enabling the
Japanese to pile up war stocks.
The Japanese have not been slow’ in building factories
to manufacture much needed instruments of war right on
the scene. Airplanes, tanks and ships all are being built in
these occupied countries.
The willingness of the natives to work for the Japanese
has enabled our enemy to build scores of airfields overnight
with native labor. This is giving Japan a great edge in pour
ing more fighting forces into tne region that one day must
be sw’ept clear by the Allied forces. It is true that Japan

still is bringing down from Formosa and from Japan much
coolie labor. But this supply of coolie labor, our military
leaders say, is much less than it w’ould have had to be, had
the natives been slow’ in responding to Japanese occupation.
It true that, even with the relatively small forces at
their disposal, American leaders are doing a grand job of
making it difficult for the Japs. In one particular case,
guerrilla fighters were landed on a Japanese occupied island
close to the Australian coast. These guerrillas sw’oop dow r n
from the hills and w’ipe oqt small garrisons and destroy
supplies and ammunition and then scatter into the hills
with comparatively small losses.
These guerrillas have been reinforced from time to
time. Supplies have been dropped to them by parachute
innumerable times. When the time comes—when we have
the instruments and the men to make the move—the task
of taking this particular Japanese stronghold will have been
made much easier because of the brilliant and daring work
of these guerrillas.
One American military leader, in discussing this phase
of operations in the South Pacific, dw’elt at length on the
stubbornness with which the Japanese are fighting.
Japs Taught How to Die
“You know,” he said, “it isn’t strange to see how the
Japanese fight to the death, when you consider their
education, or lack of it rather, and the campaign which
their leaders have been making for years to teach them
to die.
“The Japanese soldier is little better than a coolie.
Back in Japan, if he remained a coolie until he died, under
his teaching, relatives would have to conduct services for
him for 10 years to make sure that his soul passed on to
whatever heaven the Japanese have—if any.
“But the minute he becomes a soldier, this Japanese
knows that he has a free ticket for immediate admission
to his heaven if he dies gloriously in battle. They have
been told by their officers that they will be killed if taken
prisoner and if they are, they cannot go on to join their
ancestors. They know that if they die on the field of
hattle they are heroes. That, tn my opinion, is why we

title: Detroit evening times.

text:
They gave us plenty. *
Pearl Harbor... Wake.Bataan.. .Corregidor
• •. Java Sea ... Savo Island—the worst string of
defeats in America’s military history.
The Stars and Stripes hauled down on American
soil and the Rising Sun run up... captured Ameri
can marines made to play coolie and haul grinning
Jap officers in rickshas—the Sons of Heaven have
handed us the bitterest medicine this nation ever
had to swallow.
NOW—IIT’S GET 'EM!
Let’s pour it on them! Let’s shove the men, the
ships, the guns, and the planes over there in an
overwhelming, irresistible, avenging flood that will
wind up this Jap business fast, once and for all!
And let's pay for that flood with the same grim
eagerness, the same deadly purpose that our fight
ing men show in battle!
The 6th War Loan Drive is on. Let’s open our
purses—turn out our pockets—empty the sock.
Now, if never before, let’s make sure that we
buy at least one extra $100 War Bond.
From the pilot over Tokyo to the last housewife
on the Eastern Seaboard, let's all of us, one hun
dred thirty million strong, rise up and give the
Sons of Heaven ... hell!
And her* or* 6 more reasons for buying at least
an extra $100 War Bond in the 6th!
1. War Bonds aro the bast, tha safest investment In Hie
world!
2. War Bonds return you $4 for every $3 at maturity.
3. War Bonds are as convenient as cash—and increase in
value to bootl
4. War Bonds mean education for your children, security
for you, a nest egg to fell back on, come what may.
5. War Bonds help keep down the cost of living.
6. War Bonds will help win the Peace by increasing pur
chasing power after the war.
